coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent sensing system based on molecularly imprinted phase-change microcapsules and carbon quantum dots for high-efficient detection of tetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Jinghua
Liu, Huan
Wang, Yatao
Li, Jianhua
Wu, Dezhen
Wang, Xiaodong
coredata.dc:description: Aiming at enhancing the detection efficiency and identification accuracy of tetracycline under a high-temperature condition, this study focuses on an innovative fluorescent sensing system (MIP@CQD-PCM) based on molecularly imprinted phase-change microcapsules along with the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in their shell. This system was fabricated by microencapsulating n-eicosane as a phase change material (PCM) core within a CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell, followed by coating a tetracycline-templated molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) layer onto the surface of the SiO2 shell. The specific recognition sites to tetracycline molecules were finally achieved by removal of tetracycline template from the MIP layer. Comprehensive characterizations and investigations on the structure and performance of the fluorescent sensing system were given to confirm its successful fabrication in accordance to our design strategy. The resultant MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a satisfactory thermal storage capacity and phase-change cycle stability for temperature regulation and thermal management applications under a phase-change enthalpy of over 162 J/g. Most of all, a typical fluorescence-quenching effect was obtained from the combination of the CQDs embedded in the SiO2 shell and the tetracycline molecules adsorbed in the MIP layer. This makes the MIP@CQD-PCM achieve an enhanced capability for the fluorescence identification of tetracycline in a high-temperature environment through the in situ thermal management of its PCM core. The MIP@CQD-PCM also displays high selectivity and good reusability for tetracycline detection in industrial applications. This work provides a promising strategy for the design and development of fluorescent sensing systems with high recognition efficiency and identification accuracy in the detection of hazardous substances.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent sensing system based on molecularly imprinted phase-change microcapsules and carbon quantum dots for high-efficient detection of tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aiming at enhancing the detection efficiency and identification accuracy of tetracycline under a high-temperature condition, this study focuses on an innovative fluorescent sensing system (MIP@CQD-PCM) based on molecularly imprinted phase-change microcapsules along with the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) embedded in their shell. This system was fabricated by microencapsulating n-eicosane as a phase change material (PCM) core within a CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell, followed by coating a tetracycline-templated molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) layer onto the surface of the SiO2 shell. The specific recognition sites to tetracycline molecules were finally achieved by removal of tetracycline template from the MIP layer. Comprehensive characterizations and investigations on the structure and performance of the fluorescent sensing system were given to confirm its successful fabrication in accordance to our design strategy. The resultant MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a satisfactory thermal storage capacity and phase-change cycle stability for temperature regulation and thermal management applications under a phase-change enthalpy of over 162 J/g. Most of all, a typical fluorescence-quenching effect was obtained from the combination of the CQDs embedded in the SiO2 shell and the tetracycline molecules adsorbed in the MIP layer. This makes the MIP@CQD-PCM achieve an enhanced capability for the fluorescence identification of tetracycline in a high-temperature environment through the in situ thermal management of its PCM core. The MIP@CQD-PCM also displays high selectivity and good reusability for tetracycline detection in industrial applications. This work provides a promising strategy for the design and development of fluorescent sensing systems with high recognition efficiency and identification accuracy in the detection of hazardous substances.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decades, it has become a consensus that the use and disposal of medicinal substances are bound to have an impact on the environment. There is no doubt that many of medicinal substances are designed to influence the biological systems including human beings, and they can easily penetrate bio-membranes to generate persisting effectiveness [1]. As a type of essential medicinal substances, antibiotics have been widely used both by human and in the veterinarian field, which can make them easily enter the environment through a variety of routes. It is inevitable that the antibiotics from domestic sources and pharmaceutical industries are generally accumulated in food chains, soils, natural water systems, and other aquatic environments like industrial and living sewage [2,3]. However, most of antibiotics are environmentally harmful compounds. The inappropriate and excessive use of antibiotics has given rise to antibiotic pollution, resulting in the occurrence of antibiotic resistant bacteria and antibiotic resistance genes [4]. This causes long-term adverse effects on human health due to the damage of ecosystem [5]. Tetracycline is one of the most important broad-spectrum antibiotics that can be administered to treat and control infectious diseases due to its good antimicrobial effectiveness at a low cost, therefore it has been widely applied in animal husbandry, aquaculture, and breeding industry [2,6]. Meanwhile, tetracycline has been frequently detected at high concentrations in groundwater and surface water as well as wastewater treatment plants in China and Southeast Asia due to its extensive use [5,7]. Tetracycline resistance genes become a major problem arising from the pollution of tetracycline. Such a problem not only takes place in aquatic organisms but also is the most serious in the family livestock farms [8]. This allows tetracycline to easily enter the food chains of human beings. Taking into account potential environmental impacts and health risks, there is an urgent requirement for the development of innovative techniques to accurately detect and identify tetracycline in the aquatic environment.

To meet this immediate requirement, several approaches have been explored, which include high-performance liquid chromatography combined with tandem mass spectrometry, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy, capillary electrophoresis, colorimetric analysis and the assay of enzyme-linked aptamers [9–12]. In recent years, fluorescent molecular imprinting technology has been acknowledged as a powerful tool for highly sensitive detection and accurate determination of tetracycline [13]. In this technology, the molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) combined with fluorescent materials are specifically synthesized with the specific binding sites designed for target molecules. Such a combination can integrate the characteristics of MIPs and fluorescent materials to generate a fluorescence quenching behavior [14]. This not only can lead to a high affinity and high selectivity to target molecules, but also can provide operation simplicity, short incubation time, and visible recognition for the determination of these analytes through a facile fluorescent detection method [3,6]. Hou et al. [15] reported a hydrophilic–hydrophobic patterned MIP coupled with photonic crystals as a fluorescent sensor for high-sensitive colorimetric detection of tetracycline. Chao et al. [16] designed and prepared two types of silicon-based MIP composites with CdTe quantum dots. These two composites could provide highly sensitive and highly accurate fluorometric detection for tetracycline in the aquatic environment. Xu et al. [12] fabricated a fluorescent probe based on molecularly imprinted silica and graphitic carbon nitride. This fluorescent probe was successfully applied for the detection of tetracycline in dairy products. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of promising luminescent nanomaterials that can be facilely synthesized in high yield using natural organic raw materials [17,18]. CQDs are very suitable for use in fluorescent molecular imprinting systems for detection and identification of tetracycline in view of their excellent photoluminescence performance, good cytocompatibility, good biodegradation capability, superior photochemical stability, and low environmental toxicity [14,19]. Li et al. [6] reported a single-hole hollow MIP embedded with CQDs. Such a fluorescent polymer can be used for the rapid detection of tetracycline in honey. Zhang et al. [20] developed a fluorescence sensing system based on the MIP coupled with nitrogen-doped graphene-based CQDs for the highly sensitive recognition of tetracycline in the animal-derived food. Liu et al. [21] tailored a molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent sensor based a dual-emission CQDs hybrid. This sensor was successfully applied for the ultrasensitive detection of tetracycline in complicated environments. There are several factors to be taken into account when evaluating the sensitivity and detection accuracy of a fluorescent molecular imprinting system based on CQDs. Ambient temperature is considered as an important factor influencing the determination of analytes. Yang et al. [22] found that CQDs was sensitive to temperature, and they presented lower fluorescence intensity at a higher ambient temperature when used for the detection of tetracycline. Such a temperature dependency of fluorescence intensity is ascribed to the fact that more photo-excited electrons return to the ground state from the excited state due to the thermal activation of surface trap and the increased probability of nonradiative excitation recombination induced thermally [23,24]. On the other hand, a high temperature may reduce the binding affinity between MIPs and target molecules, promoting the desorption of target molecules from MIPs [25]. Both of two factors can lead to a decrease in original fluorescence intensity of the MIPs–CQDs sensing system. As a result, the detection accuracy and identification capability of the sensing system are bound to decrease when used for the determination of tetracycline in a high-temperature environment.

Phase change materials (PCMs) are a class of thermal energy storage materials that can absorb and release huge amounts of latent heat through reversible solid–liquid phase transitions [26]. Nowadays, PCMs as important renewable energy materials were extensively developed for heat energy-storage and thermal management applications in the past decades [27–29]. As an important application technology of PCMs, microencapsulation has been widely adopted to prevent PCMs from leaking and flowing away in their molten state [30,31]. Through fabricating a polymeric, inorganic or composite functional shell onto the PCM core, the resultant phase-change microcapsules not only can carry out heat energy storage and thermal management by their PCM core, but also can obtain specific physical or chemical functions from their shell materials [32,33]. This design strategy can lead to the formation of bi- or multi-functional phase-change microcapsules, making them gain multipurpose applications [34–36]. With such an idea in mind, we designed a novel fluorescent sensing system based on molecularly imprinted phase-change microcapsules along with the CQDs embedded in their shell. This design is expected to address the temperature-dependent problem in tetracycline detection through the in situ thermal management derived from the PCM core within the sensing system. This fluorescent sensing system was constructed through encapsulating a paraffin-type PCM with a CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell, followed by coating a tetracycline-templated MIP layer on the shell surface. The specific recognition sites were finally achieved in this sensing system by removal of tetracycline template from the MIP layer. The resultant microcapsules were expected to obtain a fluorescent molecular imprinting function from their functional shell in addition to a thermal energy-storage capability from their PCM core. Therefore, the fluorescent sensing system developed by the present work can make use of its PCM core to perform thermal management and temperature regulation in situ to improve the detection efficiency and identification accuracy of tetracycline in a high-temperature environment. This paper focused on the fabrication technology, microstructures, phase-change behaviors, and thermal regulation performance of the fluorescent sensing system. Its fluorescent recognition capability was particularly investigated under different ambient temperatures. This study can provide an innovative strategy for the design and construction of fluorescent sensing systems with a thermal management function to meet the requirement for high-efficient determination of hazardous substances in a wide temperature range.

N-eicosane with a purity of 99 wt% was commercially supplied by Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and used as a paraffin-type PCM. Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), citric acid (CA), acrylamide (AM), N,N-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA), potassium persulfate (KPS), γ-methacryloxypropyl trimethoxysilane (γ-MPS), polyethyleneimine (PEI), acetic acid (AA), formamide, methanol, and HCl aqueous solution with a concentration of 37.5 wt% were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. Tetracycline (TC), doxycycline, oxytetracycline, and sulfadiazine were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., China. All of reagents and raw materials were used as received without further purification.

At the start of the experiment, CQDs were synthesized through a bottom-up approach in the hydrothermal process as schematically described in Fig. 1
[37,38]. In a typical procedure, 0.3 g of CA and 0.3 g of PEI were dissolved in 5.0 mL of deionized water with stirring to obtain a homogenous solution. The resultant solution was heated in a hydrothermal reactor at 180 °C for 2 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain a yellow solution containing CQDs. The phase-change microcapsules based on an n-eicosane core and a CQDs-embedded and vinyl-functionalized SiO2 shell (designated as CQD-PCM) were prepared through interfacial polycondensation in a nonaqueous oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion templating system according to the synthetic strategy presented in Fig. 1. In a typical procedure, 14.0 g of n-eicosane as a paraffin-type PCM, 14.0 g of TEOS as a silica source, and 2.5 mL of γ-MPS as a functional silica source were mixed in a 1000-mL three-necked round-bottom flask at 45 °C with stirring for 1 h. In another beaker, 300.0 mL of formamide as a dispersion medium and 4.5 g of CTAB as a cationic surfactant were mixed with stirring for 1 h. The solution was added into the flask. The resultant mixture was emulsified with vigorous agitation for 6 h to obtain a homogenous emulsion. Subsequently, 300.0 mL of HCl aqueous solution (pH –0.25) and 10.0 mL of CQDs solution was dropwise added into the flask under agitation for 4 h. Following the completion of interfacial polycondensation, the reaction suspension was continuously aged at 50 °C for 12 h. The product was filtered, washed with deionized water, and dried at room temperature for 48 h to obtain the CQD-PCM. The microcapsules comprised of an n-eicosane core and a pure SiO2 shell (designated as SiO2-PCM) were also synthesized under the identical condition as a control without any functional groups and embedded CQDs in their shell.

Following the formation of CQD-PCM, a tetracycline-templated MIP layer was coated onto the surface of CQD-PCM through surface self-assembly and radical polymerization to obtain the desired fluorescent sensing system (designated as MIP@CQD-PCM). Fig. 2 shows the schematic synthetic route and reaction mechanism of MIP@CQD-PCM. In a typical procedure, 5.0 g of CQD-PCM, 1.6 g of CTAB as a cationic surfactant, and 250.0 mL of deionized water were mixed in a 1000-mL three-necked round-bottom flask at 30 °C with stirring for 30 min. In another beaker, 2.5 g of AM as a functional monomer, 0.16 g of tetracycline as a template, 0.25 g of MBA as a crosslinking agent, and 250.0 mL of deionized water were mixed with agitation for 10 min to obtain a homogenous solution. The resultant solution was added into the flask with stirring for 1 h. Subsequently, 80.0 mL of aqueous solution containing 3.3 g of KPS was added into the flask with stirring for 30 min. The resultant mixture was heated to 75 °C at reflux for 20 h. Following the completion of polymerization, the obtained product was cooled, filtered, and washed with an aqueous eluent of acetic acid (10.0 vol%) to remove the tetracycline template to obtain the MIP@CQD-PCM. Non-imprinted phase-change microcapsules (designated as NIP@CQD-PCM) were synthesized as a control without molecular imprinting under the identical condition. Moreover, CQDs-embedded molecularly imprinted silica spheres (designated as MIP@CQD-SPR) were also synthesized as a control without PCM core under the identical condition.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were conducted to investigate the surface morphologies and microstructures of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM using a Zeiss SUPRA™ 55 field-emission scanning electron microscope and a JEOL JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope, respectively. The fluorescence microscopic images of MIP@CQD-PCM were recorded on a Leica TCS SP8 confocal laser-scanning microscope. The surface elemental distributions of microcapsule samples were characterized with an Oxford INCAX-Act energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer. Their surface elemental composition and chemical state were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Fisher Scientific EscaLab 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer with a focused monochromatized Al–Kα radiation source. The Casa XPS software was adopted to fit the core level XPS spectra, in which a Shirley background was assumed. The Lorentzian-Gaussian peak-shape analysis was carried out to optimize the deconvolution of the core level XPS spectra. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed to analyze the chemical structures of microcapsule samples using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 infrared spectrometer by means of a KBr tablet method. Infrared spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 2 cm−1. X–ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were record at room temperature using a Japan Rigaku D/Max 2500 VB2 + P/C X–ray diffractometer with a Cu–Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54056 Å) at 40 kV and 20 mA in the 2θ range of 5°–80°. The specimen of pristine CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell was achieved by powerfully grinding the CQD-PCM sample, followed by extraction with methanol to remove the n-eicosane core. In this case, the resultant XRD pattern only reflected the diffraction characteristics of the CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell without any diffraction disturbance from the n-eicosane core.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed to analyze the phase-change behaviors and thermal properties of pure PCM and microcapsule samples using a TA Instruments Q20 differential scanning calorimeter. The measurement was carried out from 0 to 60 °C at a scanning rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. Melting peak temperature (T m), crystallization peak temperature (T c), melting enthalpy (ΔH m), and crystallization enthalpy (ΔH c) were determined from DSC thermal analysis. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted to evaluate the thermal degradation behaviors of pure PCM and microcapsule samples using a TA Instruments Q50 thermogravimetric analyzer at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The thermal management behaviors and temperature regulation performance of microcapsule samples were characterized with a Testo™ 875–1i versatile thermal imager. The surface temperature of the sample was recorded as a function of time as determined from the infrared thermographic analysis using the Testo™ ComSoft Basic software. A heat-impact experiment was carried out to evaluate the shape/form stability of microcapsule samples through isothermal heating on a high-precision electronic hot plate. The aspects of specimens were monitored in real-time by using a digital camera and their temperatures were recorded using a Fluke™ MT4 MAX handheld infrared thermometer.

A Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to measure the fluorescence performance of MIP@CQD-PCM and NIP@CQD-PCM. The slit width was set to 5 nm for both excitation and emission, and the photomultiplier tube voltage was set to 600 V. In a typical testing procedure, tetracycline was dissolved in deionized water to obtain a homogenous solution with a concentration of 100 mg/L and then stored at 4 °C for further use. The MIP@CQD-PCM was also dispersed in deionized water to obtain a homogenous suspension (100 mg/L). A series of mixtures were prepared at different volume ratios of tetracycline solution to MIP@CQD-PCM suspension. The fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded in the wavelength range of 385–700 nm at an excitation wavelength of 377 nm with a scanning speed of 1200 nm/min.

Fig. 1 shows the scheme of synthetic strategies and reaction mechanisms of CQDs and CQD-PCM. As schematically depicted in Fig. 1, CQDs were obtained from a hydrothermal reaction using CA as a carbon source and PEI as a surface passivation agent according to the bottom-up method reported in the literature [39,40]. The resultant CQDs can be clearly identified from the TEM images in Fig. 4 e and f, in which they reveal a well-defined hyperbranched structure and a homogenous particle-size distribution with a diameter of approximately 12 nm. It has been broadly reported that CQDs can generate photoluminescence due to their band-gap transition together with the electron hole recombination resulting from their surface defects [13,41]. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs into the SiO2 shell was expected to impart fluorescence performance to the CQD-PCM. An emulsion-templated polycondensation technology was adopted to synthesize the CQD-PCM according to the schematic illustration in Fig. 1. To avoid uncontrollable hydrolysis occurring in the synthetic process, a nonaqueous (O/W) emulsion system was first established to provide a template for self-assembly of TEOS as an organic silica source on the surface of n-eicosane micelles. CQDs and γ-MPS were synchronously incorporated into the system together with the addition of the silica source. This can lead to an introduction of fluorescence effectiveness and vinyl functional groups into the SiO2 shell. The hydrolysis of the self-assembled silica source was initiated by an HCl aqueous solution, and the follow-up polycondensation of silica precursor was driven by an acidic environment to fabricate a vinyl-functionalized and CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell surrounding the n-eicosane core. It should be noted that the CQD-PCM was formed with a mass ratio of silica source (TEOS) to PCM core (n-eicosane) at 1/1, because the resultant microcapsules could achieve a good balance between the shell thickness and thermal energy capacity in this proportion according to the results from our previous study [42]. Fig. 3
a–d shows the SEM images of CQD-PCM. It can be seen in Fig. 3 a and b that the as-synthesized CQD-PCM presents a regular spherical morphology with a smooth and compact surface, and their particle size could be estimated to be 3–5 μm. A typical core–shell microstructure can be observed from a broken microcapsule in the CQD-PCM sample (see Fig. 3 c). Such a well-defined core–shell structure was further identified by the TEM images in Fig. 4
a and b, from which the shell thickness of CQD-PCM was estimated to be approximately 200 nm. Moreover, the embedded CQDs can be clearly distinguished from the damaged microcapsule shell as seen in Fig. 3 d. These results confirm the successful fabrication of CQD-PCM in accordance with our design strategy. It is believed that the formation of the vinyl-functionalized and CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell can provide tight encapsulation and effective protection for the n-eicosane core, protecting against leakage and flowing way in its molten state. Furthermore, the vinyl groups on the SiO2 shell can act as a protocol to facilitate the covalent bonding between the MIP coating layer and the surface of CQD-PCM through free radical polymerization.

Following the formation of the CQD-PCM, free radical polymerization was conducted under the induction of CTAB surfactant and the templating of tetracycline using AM as a functional monomer and MBA as a crosslinking monomer. This can lead to the formation of a MIP coating layer on the surface of CQD-PCM. As described in Fig. 2, CTAB could induce monomer molecules to assemble on the surface of CQD-PCM. With the occurrence of radical polymerization among these self-assembled monomer molecules, tetracycline molecules were adsorbed by AM segments through hydrogen bonding and then anchored upon the poly(AM-co-MBA) chains, leading to the formation of a tetracycline-templated MIP layer on the surface of CQD-PCM. Finally, the MIP@CQD-PCM was formed by removal of the anchored tetracycline molecules with an AA eluent, remaining the selective recognition sites on the MIP coating layer to be ready for rebinding new tetracycline molecules. Fig. 3 e–h shows the SEM images of the resultant MIP@CQD-PCM. It is worth noting that the MIP@CQD-PCM shows a very rough surface due the presence of the MIP coating layer. Despite undergoing the polymerization process, there is no breakage or damage observed from the microstructure and morphology of MIP@CQD-PCM. The MIP layer coated on the surface of CQD-PCM can be clearly identified from a surface stripped microcapsule in the MIP@CQD-PCM sample as seen in Fig. 3 h. The MIP layer was found to stick tightly with the SiO2 shell thanks to the chemical bonding derived from surface radical polymerization. The TEM images also confirm the presence of a homogenous coating layer on the microcapsules as well as the core–shell structural integrity of MIP@CQD-PCM as observed in Fig. 4 c and d. More importantly, the fluorescence microscopic image in Fig. 4 g clearly displays fluorescent emission from the MIP@CQD-PCM due to the presence of CQDs in its SiO2 shell. These results have provided sound evidence for the successful formation of the MIP@CQD-PCM with the desired microstructure and fluorescence performance.

The chemical compositions and structures of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM were intensively investigated by EDX, FTIR, and XPS spectroscopy, with the resultant spectra presented in Figs. 5 and 6
. It can be observed in Fig. 5 a and b that both the CQD-PCM and the MIP@CQD-PCM present four typical signals corresponding to the Si, O, N, and C elements in their EDX spectra. The Si and O elements are derived from the SiO2 shell of these two microcapsule samples. Both of them were found to show a homogeneous and dense distribution in their elemental mapping images due to the formation of a SiO2-based shell as seen in the inserts of Fig. 5 a and b. It is well known that both the MIP layer and CQDs are comprised of the N and C elements, resulting in the presence of the N and C signals in the EDX spectra of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM. However, the elemental mapping images reveal a low distribution density of the N element in both samples due to its low atomic fraction in the MIP layer and CQDs. Moreover, the highly dense carbon background was observed in the elemental mapping images due to the use of a carbon-based conductive tape in the test environment. The Si, O, N, and C atoms in the shell materials of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM can also be identified by XPS as determined in the survey XPS spectra (see Fig. 6 a). However, only the characteristic signals of Si, O, and C atoms were observed in the survey XPS spectrum of SiO2-PCM as a control with a pure SiO2 shell. The presence of C-atomic signal is ascribed to the carbon background in the test environment. The atomic coordination state of the Si–O bond can further be determined from the curve fitting for the core-level XPS spectra of Si 2p and O 1 s (see Fig. 6 b and c). Both the CQD-PCM and the MIP@CQD-PCM were found to show characteristic signals for Si (154.5 eV, 2 s and 103.0 eV, 2p), O (532.5, 1 s), N (400.0, 1 s) and C (285.5 eV, 1 s) atoms in their survey XPS spectra. As for the CQD-PCM, not only the Si–O bond but also the CO, CO, CC, CC, O–CO, CN, CH, OH, NH, and –N2 bonds could be identified from its deconvoluted core-level XPS spectra of Si, O, N, and C atoms as noted in Fig. 6 d–g. This suggests the presence of the vinyl-functionalized and CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell in the CQD-PCM. In the case of the MIP@CQD-PCM, there are two new C6H5x– and –NH–CO bonds identified from its deconvoluted core-level XPS spectra of N 1 s and C 1 s except for the chemical bonds mentioned in the CQD-PCM as seen in Fig. 6 h–k. In view of a similarity in atomic coordination state between the MIP layer and CQDs, these results are considered as a proof for the formation of the MIP layer upon the CQD-PCM.

The chemical structures and functional groups of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM were further examined by using FTIR spectroscopy, with the resultant infrared spectra presented in Fig. 5 c. It is of importance to note that both the CQD-PCM and the MIP@CQD-PCM show a series of intensive absorption peaks for the n–eicosane core and SiO2 shell in their infrared spectra, in which the characteristic absorption bands of the n–eicosane core are in good agreement with those of pure n–eicosane. This confirms the successful encapsulation of n–eicosane with a SiO2-based shell. Furthermore, the infrared spectrum of MIP@CQD-PCM confirms the formation of a MIP coating layer due to the presence of the characteristic peaks for the CO, CC, N–OH, and NH stretching vibrations. These results provide supplementary evidence for the successful construction of the MIP@CQD-PCM. The crystallinity and crystal structure of the n-eicosane core within two microcapsule samples were investigated by XRD, with the resulting XRD pattern presented in Fig. 5 d. It is worth noting that the CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM exhibit almost all of the diffraction peaks of the n-eicosane crystal in their XRD patterns. These diffraction peaks are highly consistent with those of pure n-eicosane as marked in Fig. 5 d. On the other hand, the pristine CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell seems to be amorphous, because only a diffuse peak appears in its XRD pattern. These results suggest that the crystallinity and crystal structure of the n-eicosane core are not influenced by encapsulation with a CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell together with a MIP coating layer. Therefore, the phase-transition capability of the n-eicosane core within the MIP@CQD-PCM can be well maintained for future thermal management and temperature regulation applications.

Dynamic DSC scans were performed to evaluate the phase–transition behaviors and latent heat-storage performance of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM, as well as pure n–eicosane as a control. Fig. 7
a shows the resultant DSC thermograms, with the DSC thermal analysis results summarized in Fig. 7 b–d. It can be seen in Fig. 7 a and b that pure n–eicosane exhibits a bimodal crystallization behavior with two exothermic peaks at 28.5 and 30.4 °C in its cooling DSC curve, while it shows a single melting endothermic at 36.6 °C in its heating DSC curve. Such a bimodal exothermic behavior is attributed to the rotator phase transformation that commonly takes place in the case of the crystallization process of n–alkane as broadly reported in the literature [43]. N-alkanes usually experience an intermediary phase designated as a metastable rotator phase during the phase transition from liquid to a stable crystalline phase due to intra-chain defects or the formation of conformational disordered crystals [43,44]. In general, the rotator phase transition occurs at a temperature higher than the crystallization phase-transition temperature, because the melt surface freezing can weaken the intermolecular interaction and therefore induces the formation of rotator phase earlier than the stable crystalline phase [45]. This leads to a rotator-phase-transition temperature (T R) higher than the T c in the DSC cooling curve of pure n–eicosane. The normalized enthalpies for crystallization and melting phase transitions were calculated to be 248.1 and 250.2 J/g, assigned to the ΔH c and ΔH m of pure n–eicosane, respectively, as seen in Fig. 7 c. This indicates that pure n-eicosane has a high thermal storage capacity for applications in the case of thermal management as a promising organic PCM.

It should be noted that both the CQD-PCM and the MIP@CQD-PCM show similar bimodal crystallization and signal melting behaviors as pure n–eicosane in the cooling and heating DSC curves. This indicates that there is no influence on the phase transition behaviors of the n–eicosane core from the encapsulation of the SiO2-based shell and the follow-up coating of the MIP coating layer. However, a slight decrease can be found in the T c's of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM due to the geometric confinement of micro-sized capsules [46]. It is understandable that a small space within the microcapsules may depress the crystallization of the n–eicosane core, resulting in a reduction in T c. It seems that there is almost no influence on the T m's of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM, and only a very slight fluctuation can be observed as seen in Fig. 7 b. It is worth noting in Fig. 7 c that the CQD-PCM shows a decrease in ΔH c by 63.8 J/g and in ΔH m by 59.6 J/g in comparison to pure n–eicosane. Evidently, the presence of a SiO2-based shell as an inert material reduced the weight fraction of the n–eicosane core in the whole microcapsules, leading to a decrease in the phase-change enthalpies of CQD-PCM. For this reason, the follow-up fabrication of a MIP layer results in a further reduction in the ΔH c and ΔH m of MIP@CQD-PCM. Nevertheless, the phase-change enthalpies were still kept at above 160 J/g for the MIP@CQD-PCM, suggesting a satisfactory thermal storage capacity for thermal management applications. Three encapsulation parameters, encapsulation ratio (E en), encapsulation efficiency (E es) and heat energy-storage efficiency (C es), can be determined from the DSC analysis data using the equations in Fig. 7. In these equations, ΔH m,PCM and ΔH c,PCM are the melting and crystallization enthalpies of pure n–eicosane, respectively, and ΔH m, MEPCM and ΔH c, MEPCM are the melting and crystallization enthalpies of a phase-change microcapsule sample, respectively. These three encapsulation parameters reflect the thermal storage effectiveness of the n-eicosane core within the microcapsules, and their calculated results are presented in Fig. 7 d. It can be seen that the CQD-PCM achieved an E en of 77.11% and an E es of 76.74% for thermal energy storage/release through reversible solid–liquid phase transitions. The MIP@CQD-PCM shows a further reduction in E en and E es by 68.87% and 68.21%, respectively, in the presence of the MIP layer. This indicates that the thermal storage efficiency of phase-change microcapsules is inversely proportional to the weight fraction of their shell materials. However, both the CQD-PCM and the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibit an extremely high C es of over 99%, which means that more than 99% of microencapsulated n-eicosane can effectively perform thermal energy storage and release through its reversible phase transitions.

The phase-change reversibility and repeatability of MIP@CQD-PCM were evaluated by dynamic DSC scans with a heating–cooling thermal cycle for 100 times in the temperature range of −10–80 °C. Fig. 8
a shows the resultant DSC thermograms at an interval of cycle number of 10 times, with the corresponding thermal analysis data presented in Fig. 8 b and c. It is important to note in Fig. 8 a that these DSC curves show highly consistent profiles with little variation in the position and intensity of their exothermic and endothermic peaks from the first cycle to the last. Furthermore, the MIP@CQD-PCM was found to present a very small fluctuation of only ± 0.24 °C in its T c and ± 0.21 °C in its T m over the across all of the DSC thermal cycles as seen in Fig. 8 b. Its ΔH c and ΔH m also fluctuated slightly in a very narrow range of ± 0.56 J/g during the thermal cycles (see Fig. 8 c). These results clearly demonstrate a resilient and reversible solid–liquid phase-transition behavior of the MIP@CQD-PCM. This means that the MIP@CQD-PCM can repeatedly perform latent heat absorption and release at almost constant temperatures with little loss in phase-change enthalpies. Therefore, the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a good working durability and reusability in thermal management application when used for detection and determination of tetracycline.

The temperature regulation behavior and thermal management performance of MIP@CQD-PCM were investigated by means of an infrared thermographic technology, with the MIP@CQD-SPR evaluated as a control without PCM core. These two samples were synchronously heated up to 70 °C under an isothermal condition, followed by natural cooling down to room temperature. Their surface temperatures were monitored in real-time with an infrared thermographic camera. Fig. 9
a and b shows a set of representative infrared thermographic images recorded for these two samples in the heating and cooling processes. Fig. 9 c and d shows the corresponding plots of quantitative surface temperature evolution as a function of time. As noted in Fig. 9 a and b, the MIP@CQD-SPR displays a continuous and distinct change in color from blue to orange in the heating process and then from red to blue in the cooling process. The plot of temperature evolution with heating time reveals that the surface temperature of MIP@CQD-SPR rises up rapidly without any delaying during the heating process (see Fig. 9 c). There is also a continuous decrease in its surface temperature without any hysteresis until the sample was cooled down to room temperature as seen in Fig. 9 d. It is well known that the total heat energy absorption/release of MIP@CQD-SPR is only dominated by its sensible heat in the absence of PCM core [47]. Therefore, the MIP@CQD-SPR presents a fast thermal response to the variation of background temperature due to its high heat transfer efficiency.

In contrast to the MIP@CQD-SPR, the MIP@CQD-PCM was found to show a delayed color change in both the heating and the cooling processes as observed from its infrared thermographic images in Fig. 9 a and b. It is of interest to note in Fig. 9 c and d that visible temperature hysteresis appears in the plots of temperature evolution with the heating and cooling time. Furthermore, such temperature hysteresis was found to take place in the ranges of approximately 32.2–43.5 °C and 28.2–37.4 °C during the heating and cooling processes, respectively. These two temperature-hysteresis regions are almost consistent with the temperature ranges of the n-eicosane core for fusion and solidification as seen in Fig. 7 a. It is understandable that in the case of the MIP@CQD-PCM, not only the sensible heat but also the latent heat involved the total thermal energy absorption and release due to the presence of a PCM core. The quantity of latent heat is commonly several times greater than that of sensible heat. In this case, the MIP@CQD-PCM can absorb and release huge amounts of extra thermal energy through the phase transitions of its n-eicosane core. Such extra thermal energy absorption and release normally occurs at an almost constant temperature. This can generate a thermal buffering effect on the microcapsule system, leading to a thermal response delay occurring with the resultant temperature hysteresis as exhibited by the MIP@CQD-PCM. These results confirm that the MIP@CQD-PCM can carry out effective temperature regulation in situ for the local microenvironment when used for detection and recognition of tetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and high-temperature impact resistance
ce:section.ce:para:
TGA was conducted to evaluate the thermal stabilities of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM under a nitrogen atmosphere, with pure n-eicosane examined as a control sample. The resultant TGA and DTG thermograms are presented in Fig. 10
a and b, respectively. It can be seen in Fig. 10 a that pure n-eicosane shows a typical one-step thermal degradation behavior in its TGA curve with rapid weight loss taking place in the temperature range of approximately 125–230 °C. Such a drastic weight loss is attributed to the evaporation of n-eicosane, leaving little residual char at the end of TGA test. The characteristic temperature at the maximum weight loss rate (T max) is commonly used as an important index to indicate the thermal stability of a material [48], as determined from the DTG thermogram. Pure n-eicosane was found to reveal a T max of 215.8 °C according to its DTG thermogram presented in Fig. 10 b. It is important to note that the CQD-PCM shows a similar one-step thermal degradation behavior as pure n-eicosane, and however its char yield is dramatically increased to 24.2 wt%. This residual char represents the indecomposable wall materials retained at the end of the TGA test, and its yield is strongly associated with the encapsulation ratio of the CQD-PCM mentioned in Fig. 7 d. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the CQD-PCM shows a significant increase in T max by 33.9 °C in comparison to pure n-eicosane. There is no doubt that the encapsulation of the SiO2-based shell can generate a good sealing effect on the n-eicosane core, preventing its evaporation in the heating process. This leads to a delay of thermal degradation process along with a considerable improvement in T max. In contrast to the CQD-PCM, the MIP@CQD-PCM was observed to exhibit a two-step degradation behavior in its TGA curve as shown in Fig. 10 a. The first stage degradation is attributed to the evaporation of the n-eicosane core and the second one is associated with the thermal decomposition of the MIP layer as a polymeric material. Consequently, the MIP@CQD-PCM presents two T max's assigned to these two different degradation stages as seen in Fig. 10 b. It is interesting to note that the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a further increase in its first T max by 5.6 °C in comparison to the CQD-PCM. Such a slight improvement is due to the presence of the MIP coating layer upon the SiO2-based shell, providing an extra heat protection for the n-eicosane core. Therefore, the MIP@CQD-PCM possesses a better thermal stability than the CQD-PCM for thermal management applications over a broad applicable temperature range.

The high-temperature impact resistance of CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM was investigated by means of an isothermal heat impact experiment at 120 °C using a simple custom-designed setup as schematically shown in Fig. 10 c. Fig. 10 c also shows a series of digital photographs recorded at different heat stages for the CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-PCM as well as pure n-eicosane as a control sample. There is no doubt that pure n-eicosane has a high fluidity in its molten state. This makes pure n-eicosane easy to lose its original form and then flow away with heating up to its melting temperature as observed in Fig. 10 c. Therefore, pure n-eicosane is not recommended for use in thermal management due to a difficulty to handle in the molten state. In contrast to pure n-eicosane, both the CQD-PCM and the MIP@CQD-PCM were found to always keep their original form and shape until heated up to 120 °C. There is no leakage and permeation of the n-eicosane core occurring during the isothermal heating process. This indicates that the SiO2-based shell can well isolate the n-eicosane core from the surrounding environment across a large temperature range, effectively protecting against its leakage and permeation in the molten state. Moreover, the MIP layer can provide an extra protection for the n-eicosane core thanks to its higher thermal decomposition temperature as confirmed by TGA. Therefore, the MIP@CQD-PCM presents better high-temperature impact resistance and form/shape stability to meet the requirement for the use in temperature regulation and thermal management below an upper operation temperature of 120 °C.

The fluorescence detection performance of MIP@CQD-PCM was characterized by fluorescent spectroscopy at an excitation wavelength of 377 nm. Fig. 11
a shows the fluorescent spectra of MIP@CQD-PCM before and after the adsorption of tetracycline, in which an intensive fluorescence peak appears at an emission wavelength of 437 nm. The MIP@CQD-PCM was found to present high fluorescence intensity in the absence of tetracycline. It is of importance to note that its fluorescence intensity decreases drastically after adsorbing tetracycline. This can be attributed to the fluorescence quenching occurring between the adsorbed tetracycline and embedded CQDs in the MIP@CQD-PCM according to a mechanism of inner filter effect [13]. Fig. 11 b shows the UV absorption spectrum of tetracycline and the fluorescence spectra of MIP@CQD-PCM. It can be seen that there is an overlap for the UV absorption spectrum of tetracycline with the excitation and/or emission spectra of the MIP@CQD-PCM. This confirms the inner filter effect that was generated in the current detection system. As a result, a significant change in fluorescence intensity caused by the inner filter effect can be utilized to detect the existence of tetracycline. The fluorescence intensity of a tetracycline detection system is greatly affected by the concentration of MIP@CQD-PCM. Such a quantitive influence is given in Fig. 11 c. It is well known that detection sensitivity and linear range are two important factors for a detection system based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism [49]. Detection sensitivity can be determined from the reduction rate of fluorescence intensity designated as [(F 0–F)/F 0], where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the detection system without and with tetracycline, respectively. Normally, the greater the reduction rate, the higher is the detection sensitivity. As observed in Fig. 11 c, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system was found to exhibit an almost linear increase with the concentration of MIP@CQD-PCM. It can also be seen that at low concentrations of MIP@CQD-PCM (1.0–5.0 mg/L), a small quantity of tetracycline can result in the fluorescence quenching. Although high detection sensitivity is achieved in this concentration range, the detection system presents a narrow linear range. However, when the concentration of MIP@CQD-PCM is higher than 5.0 mg/L, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system tends to increase with a wider linear range. To obtain a reasonable balance between detection sensitivity and linear range, the optimum concentration of MIP@CQD-PCM was determined to be 9.7 mg/L through the point at which the two curves intersect as shown in Fig. 11 c.

To explore an optimum pH for a tetracycline detection system, the effect of pH on its fluorescence intensity was investigated. Fig. 11 d shows the plots of fluorescence intensity versus pH value for the tetracycline detection system. It can be observed that the detection system shows low fluorescence intensity in an acidic medium. This may be ascribed to the protonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs under the acidic condition [50]. However, its fluorescence intensity tends to increase with an increase in pH and reaches a maximum at pH 7.0. On the other hand, the detection system presents high fluorescence intensity in an alkaline medium due to the deprotonation of the surface functional groups, leading to the formation of a negative charged protective layer. Such a protective layer can protect against the alkali attack to CQDs, promoting high fluorescence intensity for the detection system under the alkali condition [51]. Fig. 11 e presents the effects of UV exposure time and incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of the detection system. It should be noted that the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits stable fluorescence intensity with a limited fluctuation at different UV exposure time. This suggests that the embedded CQDs have been well protected by the SiO2 shell material, and therefore they can generate stable sensing effectiveness for the MIP@CQD-PCM when used for tetracycline detection. To determine optimum incubation time for the detection system, the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of MIP@CQD-PCM by tetracycline was investigated by using a standard suspension containing 9.7 mg/L MIP@CQD-PCM and 10 mg/L tetracycline. As seen in Fig. 11 e, the MIP@CQD-PCM shows a stable value in its fluorescence intensity with incubation time of over 35 min. This means that it takes at least 35 min for the MIP@CQD-PCM to adsorb tetracycline molecules through the selective recognition sites inside the MIP layer [52]. It is for this reason that the optimum incubation time could be determined to be 35 min for this tetracycline detection system.

The identification capability of MIP@CQD-PCM was evaluated under the optimum detection condition determined above, with the NIP@CQD-PCM examined as a control without molecular imprinting. Fig. 12 shows the fluorescence spectra of MIP@CQD-PCM and NIP@CQD-PCM suspensions along with the corresponding Stern-Volmer plots obtained from linear fitting for fluorescence spectroscopic results. It can be observed in Fig. 12 a and b that the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a more significant reduction in fluorescence intensity than the NIP@CQD-PCM with an increase in the concentration of tetracycline, indicating a better fluorescence-quenching effect. This can be explained by the fact that the MIP@CQD-PCM has a stronger binding capacity and higher sensitivity to tetracycline thanks to the high-efficient selective adsorption for tetracycline through its molecular imprinting recognition sites. The fluorescence-quenching effect can be quantitively estimated by fluorescence-quenching rate (F 0/F) using the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1)

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the detection system in the absence and presence of tetracycline, respectively, K sv represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and C is the concentration of tetracycline. It is interesting to note in Fig. 12 c and d that there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence-quenching rate and tetracycline concentration for both the MIP@CQD-PCM and the NIP@CQD-PCM over a range of concentration from 0 to 27 mg/L. The linear fitting equations of MIP@CQD-PCM and NIP@CQD-PCM were determined as follows:

The imprinting factor, defined as the ratio of K SV, MIP to K SV, NIP, can be employed to evaluate the selective recognition capability of MIP@CQD-PCM. The imprinting factor of MIP@CQD-PCM was calculated to be 2.94, indicating a good recognition and rebinding capability for tetracycline molecules. Fig. 13
a and b shows the comparative digital photographs of MIP@CQD-PCM and NIP@CQD-PCM suspensions at different concentrations of tetracycline under the 365-nm UV exposure. It is interestingly seen in Fig. 13 a that the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a visible change in the blue-emission fluorescence from light to dark with increasing the concentration of tetracycline. Such a drastic fluorescence reduction is attributed to the adsorption of tetracycline in the MIP layer, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. On the other hand, the NIP@CQD-PCM only adsorbs very few tetracycline molecules in the absence of specific recognition sites, resulting in a poor fluorescence-quenching effect. Therefore, the digital photographs of NIP@CQD-PCM display no apparent change in fluorescence as seen in Fig. 13 b. These results suggest that the MIP@CQD-PCM is possible to be used for the visible fluorescence detection and identification of tetracycline.

To confirm the effect of thermal management on the fluorescence identification effectiveness of a tetracycline detection system, we carried out a comparative investigation on the fluorescence intensity of MIP@CQD-PCM at a fixed concentration of tetracycline but different operation temperatures, with the MIP@CQD-SPR examined as a control without PCM core. Fig. 14 presents the effect of operation temperature on the fluorescence identification rates of MIP@CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-SPR. It is of importance to note that although these two samples show an evident temperature-dependent fluorescence-quenching behavior, they present different variation trends in fluorescence quenching rate with operation temperature as seen in Fig. 14 a. The fluorescence-quenching rate of MIP@CQD-PCM was found to jump up at operation temperatures above 38 °C. However, the MIP@CQD-SPR only shows an almost linear increase in fluorescence-quenching rate with operation temperature. It has been reported that a high temperature is disadvantageous to the selective adsorption of targeted molecules by a MIP due to weakening of the electrostatic interaction between them [53]. On the other hand, the tetracycline detection mechanism involved with CQDs can be attributed to the internal filter effect [54]. Therefore, CQDs also have the temperature dependency of fluorescence derived from photo-excited electrons. With a rise in temperature, more electrons return to the ground state from the excited state, leading to the weakening of fluorescence intensity. On the contrary, a low temperature can help electrons jump from the ground state to the excited state, enhancing the fluorescence intensity with a decrease in temperature accordingly [22]. It has been known that there is a melting phase transition occurring in the n-eicosane core of MIP@CQD-PCM at temperatures above its melting temperature. Such a phase transition behavior can result in a reduction in the temperature of the surrounding microenvironment of MIP@CQD-PCM through fusion heat absorption. As a result, the MIP@CQD-PCM can achieve a fluorescence enhancement thanks to the in situ thermal management from its n-eicosane core. In contrast, the MIP@CQD-SPR cannot obtain such a fluorescence enhancement at high temperatures due to the lack of extra thermal energy absorption for thermal management. The in situ thermal management also allows the MIP@CQD-PCM to gain a higher fluorescence-quenching rate than the MIP@CQD-SPR with an increase in incubation time as seen in Fig. 14 b. In addition, Fig. 14 c and d demonstrates that the MIP@CQD-PCM and MIP@CQD-SPR have almost the same fluorescence-quenching rate at a low temperature. However, the MIP@CQD-PCM presents a higher fluorescence-quenching rate at a high temperature thanks to the thermal management from its n-eicosane core. This means that the MIP@CQD-PCM needs shorter incubation time than the MIP@CQD-SPR to achieve a good identification capability in a high-temperature environment. These results can also be confirmed by the digital photographs and infrared thermographic images shown in Fig. 13 c and d, in which the MIP@CQD-PCM visibly displays a considerable enhancement in fluorescence quenching at high temperatures. Table 1 summarized the technical superiority and novelty of the detection system developed by this study compared to other fluorescence detection methods reported in the literature [6,13,14,51,54]. It can be seen that only our work provided the smart integration of thermal management and fluorescence detection, whereas the other reported studies never got involve in the temperature control for the fluorescence detection system. In this sense, the MIP@CQD-PCM possesses higher detection efficiency and identification accuracy for tetracycline at high temperatures.

The selectivity of MIP@CQD-PCM to tetracycline was assessed by measuring their fluorescence-quenching effectiveness for tetracycline, doxycycline and oxytetracycline as structural analogues, and sulfadiazine as a co-existing compound, with the NIP@CQD-PCM examined as a control without molecular imprinting. Fig. 15
a shows the measurement results from fluorescence spectroscopy. It is worth noting that the MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits far higher fluorescence-quenching effectiveness for tetracycline than for the other three compounds, indicating a highly selective recognition capability for tetracycline. This may be due to the difference in molecular structure between the other three compounds and tetracycline, limiting them entering the specific recognition sites of MIP@CQD-PCM. In contrast, the NIP@CQD-PCM presents a poor recognition capability for all of the four compounds due to the absence of specific recognition sites. In addition, sulfadiazine cannot be adsorbed either by the MIP@CQD-PCM or by the NIP@CQD-PCM due to its completely nonanalogous structure with the other three compounds. Reusability is one of the most important factors for the industrial applications of a fluorescence molecular imprinting detection system, because the multicycle use of the detection system can effectively reduce the detection cost [49]. The reusability of MIP@CQD-PCM was examined through an elution-rebinding cycle experiment for the MIP@CQD-PCM suspensions with tetracycline. Fig. 15 b shows the fluorescence intensity obtained from the MIP@CQD-PCM suspensions before and after the adsorption of tetracycline with an elution-rebinding cycle at least for 10 times. It can be seen that there is almost no loss in the fluorescence intensity of MIP@CQD-PCM suspensions after 10 cycles of adsorption–desorption experiments. This indicates that the MIP@CQD-PCM has stable fluorescence quenching effectiveness for repeatable use in the detection of tetracycline at least 10 times without a reduction in identification capacity.

To examine the practicability and feasibility of the MIP@CQD-PCM for the tetracycline identification, an actual water sample was sampled from a local river in Beijing, and then a spike-and-recovery experiment was conducted to assay the water specimens spiked with different concentration of tetracycline using the current detection system. The obtained assay results were summarized in Table 2 . It is found that this detection system achieved the quantitive recoveries ranging from 101.2% to 104.1%, and its relative standard deviation (RSD) from the five repeated tests for each specimen was in the range of 0.68–1.22%. These results indicate that the detection system based on the MIP@CQD-PCM developed in this work has good reliability and exhibits potential applications for rapid and accurate identification and determination of tetracycline in the aquatic environment.

In summary, an innovative fluorescent sensing system based on the molecularly imprinted phase-change microcapsules and CQDs was designed to enhance the detection efficiency and identification accuracy for tetracycline under a high-temperature condition through in situ thermal management by its PCM core. The MIP@CQD-PCM was successfully fabricated by microencapsulating n-eicosane as a PCM core with a CQDs-embedded SiO2 shell through emulsion-templated interfacial polycondensation, followed by coating a tetracycline-templated MIP layer onto the surface of the SiO2-based shell via surfactant-assisted radical polymerization. The specific recognition sites to tetracycline molecules were finally achieved for the fluorescent sensing system by removal of tetracycline template from its MIP layer. The resultant MIP@CQD-PCM presents a well-defined core–shell microstructure, uniform particle size, and regular spherical morphology, together with the desired chemical structures and functional groups as confirmed by various spectroscopic characterizations. All of these were considered to indicate the successful fabrication of MIP@CQD-PCM in accordance to our design strategy. The MIP@CQD-PCM exhibits a satisfactory thermal storage capacity for effective temperature regulation under a phase-change enthalpy of over 162 J/g. It also demonstrates good phase-change cycle stability and high-temperature impact resistance for long-term thermal management applications. More importantly, the MIP@CQD-PCM not only achieved a significant fluorescence-quenching effect derived from the embedded CQDs and the adsorbed tetracycline molecules, but also exhibited an enhanced capability for the fluorescence identification of tetracycline in a high-temperature environment thanks to the in situ thermal management of its PCM core. In addition, the MIP@CQD-PCM displays high selectivity and good reusability for the analysis and detection of tetracycline in industrial applications. This work provides a promising strategy for the design and development of fluorescent sensing systems with high recognition efficiency and identification accuracy in the detection of hazardous substances.

Jinghua Yu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Huan Liu: Visualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yatao Wang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Jianhua Li: Investigation, Methodology. Dezhen Wu: Resources, Validation, Supervision. Xiaodong Wang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.
